clues: This was discovered accidentally by a second year medical student, Jay McLean, at Johns Hopkins University in 1916 who was working with dog liver. The name is also derived from this discovery
The global supply chain is suprisingly fragile because it is extracted almost entirely from the intestinal mucosa of pigs. It takes roughly 2,000 pigs to yield a single kilogram of this medication
This medication acts by binding to the plasma protein antithrombin

In the 16th to 17th century, the first attempts at this were through using a tobacco enema to help drowning victims. This technique was heavily promoted and in Venice, was legally required with bystanders and officials facing fines if they failed to perform the enema when appropriate

An young woman drowned in the Seine and was never identified. She was nicknamed 'the Mona Lisa of the Seine' for her serene expression and a Norwegian toy manufacturer used her face for a doll used to train people in this procedure

Michael Jackson took a class in this which inspired the famous lyric 'Annie, are you okay' from his song 'Smooth Criminal' since you're taught to check for responsiveness. You can also sing the song in your head while doing this procedure since it has a tempo between 100-120 bpm, although you might prefer 'Staying Alive' or 'Another One Hits the Dust'

In the middle ages, grain contaminated with the Claviceps purpurea fungus caused catastrophic poisoning outbreaks. Victims suffered intense burning sensations, gangrene, and hallucinations. Scientists isolated this key alkaloid in 1918 which became a standard, highly effective treatment
With intense vasoconstrictive properties, this was first introduced as a migraine treatment based on the theory that migraines were caused by dilated blood vessels

This medication is frequently combined with caffeine in oral and rectal formulations such as Cafergot or Migergot to treat migraines because it dramatically improves the absorption in the digestive tract

In 2015, researchers recreated a 10th century recipe from 'Bald's Leechbook' consisting of a mixture of garlic, leek, wine, and oxgall (cow bile) brewed in a brass vessel for nine days. This was surprisingly about 90% effective at killing this biofilms from this bacteria
You probably have heard of Alexander Fleming's experiments where he found that a patch of mold was killing the bacteria around it, leading to the discovery of penicillin. What was that bacteria?
The name of this opportunistic bacteria comes from the Greek words meaning 'bunch of grapes' and 'berry' for the way cells cluster together.

This organism was first observed in 1909 by Brazilian physician Carlos Chagas, who mistakenly identified the cysts as part of the life cycle of Trypanosoma cruzi, the parasite responsible for Chagas disease
Following WWII, outbreaks of a fatal pneumonia caused by this erupted across European orphanages affecting premature and malnourished infants

On June 5, 1981, the CDC published about five cases of pneumonia caused by this fungus in young, previously healthy gay men in LA. this was the first documented public record of what would later be known as AIDS
